module_map.responsibility: HTTP protocol layer: request parsing, query strings, cookies, file uploads, response objects, status codes, content types. Provides HttpRequest and HttpResponse base classes.
WSGI/ASGI request dispatch pipeline: loads middleware chain, invokes views, manages request/response lifecycle. Entry point for web servers. Handles async/sync adaptation.
URL routing and pattern matching: parses urlpatterns, matches request paths to views, resolves view callables. Contains URLPattern, URLResolver, and regex/route pattern matching.
Generic and class-based views: View base class, dispatch logic, context rendering, template response handling. Defines HTTP method handlers (get, post, etc).
ORM layer: database abstraction, connection pooling, QuerySet API, model definition, migrations. Handles SQL generation, query execution, transaction management.
Request/response processing hooks: security (CSRF, XSS), caching, gzip compression, locale handling, common operations. Middleware components execute before/after view dispatch.
Template engine: parsing, rendering, filters, tags, template inheritance. Converts template strings to HTML with context data. Supports multiple engines.
Global configuration management: settings registry, app registry, signal dispatch. Central access point for INSTALLED_APPS, MIDDLEWARE, database config.
Application registry and app configuration: app discovery, app ready signals, model registration. Maps app labels to AppConfig instances.
Signal/event system: sender/receiver registry, signal emission, connection/disconnection. Used for request_started, request_finished, post_save hooks.

pipeline_trace.description: Web server calls WSGIHandler.__call__(environ, start_response). Environ dict contains HTTP metadata. Creates HttpRequest from environ.
Sends request_started signal. Initializes middleware chain by calling load_middleware(). Middleware instances wrap each other forming nested callables.
Each middleware's __call__ or process_request executes. Examples: CommonMiddleware (URL rewrites), CsrfViewMiddleware (token validation), GZipMiddleware. Can return response early.
URL resolver matches request.path against urlpatterns. URLResolver recursively matches patterns, returns URLPattern with matched view callable and kwargs.
Handler calls resolved view callable with request and URL kwargs. If View subclass, calls View.as_view() wrapper which calls dispatch(request, *args, **kwargs).
View.dispatch() routes to HTTP method handler (get, post, etc). Handler may query database via QuerySet, render template, or return redirect response.
QuerySet.get(), .filter(), .all() etc. trigger SQL generation and execution. Connection pooled from django.db.connections. Transaction context applied. Results hydrated to Model instances.
Template.render(context) processes template string with filters, tags, inheritance. Returns HTML string. Nested template.Template calls resolve includes and inheritance.
Handler receives HttpResponse. Middleware process_response/process_template_response hooks execute in reverse order. Each can modify response headers, content, status.
key_types: HttpResponse with content, headers, cookies, status_code

pipeline_trace.description: WSGIHandler formats response status, headers as WSGI format. Calls start_response(status, headers). Returns response body iterator or file wrapper for streaming.

cross_module_hubs.reason: Central dispatch orchestrator: imports http (request/response), urls (resolving), middleware (chain), db (transaction context), conf (settings), dispatch (signals), views (view lookup). All requests funnel through BaseHandler.get_response(). Controls middleware stack initialization and execution order.

Request/response abstraction: imported by handlers, views, middleware, template responses, forms, file uploads. HttpRequest and HttpResponse are foundational types that flow through entire pipeline. Most modules either consume or produce http objects.

Global settings registry: depended on by handlers (MIDDLEWARE, DEBUG), db (DATABASE config), middleware (various feature flags), template (TEMPLATES, installed engines), apps (INSTALLED_APPS). Settings.configure() must be called before module initialization. Central source of truth for runtime configuration.

change_proposal.feature_summary: Add an async-compatible structured logging middleware that captures per-request metadata (view name, ORM query count, response time, request path) and emits as structured JSON log entry with optional correlation ID support.
files_to_modify: django/db/utils.py (hook into query execution to count)
new_types: RequestMetadata(TypedDict) - holds view_name, query_count, response_time_ms, status_code, path, method
StructuredLogEntry(TypedDict) - final JSON-serializable log event with timestamp, correlation_id, metadata
QueryCounter (context var or thread-local) - tracks DB query count per request
change_proposal.pattern_to_follow: Follow MiddlewareMixin pattern: implement __init__(get_response), __call__(request), support both sync and async via sync_capable=True, async_capable=True attributes. Use existing middleware.common.CommonMiddleware as reference for process_request/process_response hooks. Use django.utils.log.log_response() pattern for structured output.
change_proposal.integration_point: Register in settings.MIDDLEWARE between SecurityMiddleware and other middleware (before exception handling for accurate counts). Attach view name during View.dispatch() via request.resolver_match.view_name. Hook db.connections.queries list to count ORM operations. Use django.utils.timezone.now() for timing. Emit via Python logging module with custom 'django.structured' logger.
risks: Query counting overhead: Iterating django.db.connections.queries adds non-trivial CPU cost on high-QPS apps. Mitigation: Add feature flag in settings to disable in production if needed, or use connection-level query event hooks instead.

Async compatibility: contextvars.ContextVar for QueryCounter must be async-safe. Middleware must set sync_capable/async_capable correctly or it fails in async mode. Risk of incorrect counts if context vars not properly propagated in async code.

Middleware ordering sensitivity: If registered after exception handlers, query counts on 500 errors may be incomplete. If registered after static file serving middleware, static requests pollute logs unnecessarily. Recommend clear documentation on positioning.

Memory leak potential: If QueryCounter context var not cleaned up, requests under high concurrency may accumulate state. Ensure cleanup in finally block or use contextvars.copy_context() for isolation.

Performance on large response bodies: Logging metadata for every request adds serialization (JSON encode) and I/O (write to logger). On high-traffic apps (1000+ req/s), this can measurably impact throughput. Mitigation: Sample requests or use async logging.
Backward compatibility: Adding new middleware to default MIDDLEWARE tuple could change timing behavior for existing apps. Recommendation: Keep opt-in via feature flag, not auto-enabled by default.

tool_usage.params: directory structure exploration (find, ls) and import analysis (grep patterns in middleware, handlers, db modules)
pattern /tmp/benchmark-repos/django/django/*.py to list top-level python modules
key files: handlers/wsgi.py (120 lines), handlers/base.py (100 lines), middleware/common.py (80 lines), views/generic/base.py (50 lines) to understand class hierarchy and import structure
searched for class definitions (^class), import patterns (from django), middleware structure to identify key types and cross-module dependencies
